message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 28
Recent Green phase: No code change — "sword + 3 runes → insurance sum 1750" passed immediately, confirming insurance sum uses per-component insurance value (250 each) and the block discount only affects the premium base, not the insurance sum.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. No code changed this cycle; document if nothing is warranted.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Now let me run the tests to confirm the current state, and check for the metric tooling available.

content.text:
All 28 tests pass. The Green phase confirmed no code change was needed. Let me evaluate the implementation for refactoring opportunities.

## Refactoring Evaluation

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing all function names against the latest test (insurance sum for sword + 3 runes block):

- `insuranceSum`, `insuranceValue` — clear; reveal that they compute the insured-value total per component, independent of the block discount. The latest test confirms this naming holds: `insuranceSum` correctly ignores the block discount that only affects premium base.
- `baseForItems`, `baseForType`, `unitBase`, `componentBase` — these reveal the premium-base computation, where the block discount lives. Names cleanly separate the two domains (premium vs. insurance).
- `lookupByType` — the shared helper used by both `unitBase` and `insuranceValue`. The name accurately describes its dispatch behavior: pick the component value for component types, otherwise look up in a main-item table.

Assessment: All names already reveal intent. No rename warranted.

### Step 2: Initial APP Mass (focus on the area touched this cycle)

The relevant insurance-sum path:

```
insuranceValue:  bindings(type) 1 + invocation(lookupByType) 2 + bindings(2 args) 2 + constant 1 = 6
insuranceSum:    bindings(items,sum,item) 3 + loop(reduce) 5 + invocations(reduce, +, insuranceValue, .type) ≈ 8 + constant(0) 1 = 17
```

No mutation/assignment, no conditional in this path. Mass is already minimal for the functionality.

### Step 3: Four Rules of Simple Design

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 28 pass. Confirmed green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** Names are descriptive and self-documenting. The split between premium-base helpers and insurance-value helpers is clear. No improvement found.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** I examined the two type-dispatch sites — `unitBase`/`insuranceValue` both delegate to the shared `lookupByType(type, componentValue, table)`. The conceptual duplication (component-vs-main-item dispatch) is already extracted into a single function. The two `Record<string, number>` tables (`BASE_PREMIUMS`, `INSURANCE_VALUES`) represent genuinely distinct knowledge (premium prices vs. insured values), so consolidating them would be false DRY and would hurt clarity (Rule 2 trumps Rule 3). No duplication to remove.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** No unused abstractions. `lookupByType` earns its place by serving two callers. Each constant maps to a domain concept named in the spec/tests. No element is removable without losing clarity.

### Step 4–5: Implementation / Mass Change

No change applied — every candidate change would either be a no-op or would reduce clarity. The insurance-sum code added/confirmed this cycle already reuses the existing `lookupByType` helper, so the new behavior introduced zero duplication and required zero structural cleanup.

### Step 6: Decision

This cycle's Green phase added no code (the test passed immediately because `insuranceSum` already used per-component values via the shared `lookupByType` helper). Reviewing the whole implementation against the Four Rules:

- Naming already reveals intent across both the premium and insurance domains.
- The type-dispatch duplication was already extracted into `lookupByType`; the two value tables encode distinct knowledge and must stay separate.
- Mass is minimal for the behavior; no conditionals/loops/assignments can be removed without loss of clarity.

No refactoring is warranted this cycle.

I confirmed this isn't an avoidance of refactoring — I traced each of the four rules to a concrete finding in `src/claim-office.ts` and verified the relevant code already satisfies them. Tests were re-run to establish the green baseline before evaluation.

## Report

```
Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: none warranted this cycle (documented)
  - Naming: insuranceSum / insuranceValue / lookupByType already reveal intent
  - Duplication: type-dispatch already extracted to lookupByType; the two
    value tables (BASE_PREMIUMS vs INSURANCE_VALUES) are distinct knowledge
  - Mass: insurance-sum path already minimal (~17), no conditional/assignment
    to remove
Mass Change: unchanged (no edit)
Tests: 28 passing (13 todo) ✅
```

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_05-27-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

The Green phase introduced no new code (sword + 3 runes insurance-sum test passed immediately because `insuranceSum` already sums per-component insurance values via the shared `lookupByType` helper, and the block discount is correctly confined to the premium-base path in `componentBase`). The existing structure already satisfies all four rules of simple design, so no edit was made. All 28 tests remain green.
